pairs.a_text: According to Alex Hormozi, the decision to extend trust should be based on two independent criteria: (1) does the other party have a track record of protecting what they have been given, and (2) does betraying you cost them more than protecting you, i.e., is burning you a bad deal for them? pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi argues that compensation is proportional to perceived risk assumed, not to intelligence or work ethic: the market overcompensates those whom the market perceives as taking on large risk, even when the actual risk for the skilled actor is far lower than perceived.

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi claims that all meaningful upside in life is located on the other side of uncertainty and delay, and these are the only two prices the market systematically refuses to pay. Because both uncertainty (not knowing the outcome) and delay (not knowing how long one must persist) feel unbearable to most people, they represent the primary source of arbitrage for anyone willing to bear them.

pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi asserts that AI will not change the fundamental rules of business (what makes a good offer, what drives sales, how organizations scale) but will change the tools available to competitors, increasing output leverage — and frames the transition as man-plus-better-tools vs. man-plus-better-tools, the same competitive dynamic that has always existed.

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi argues that growing a done-for-you biz-op portfolio to scale produces no recoverable equity value because the underlying businesses being built lack enterprise value; a service provider who creates significant economic value for a biz-op client cannot monetize that value through equity stakes.
pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi argues that done-for-you business-opportunity businesses generate no enterprise value in the underlying client businesses because those businesses are themselves built on a legally questionable biz-op model, making equity stakes in them worthless.

The specific percentage is irrelevant; what matters is that three compensation scenarios are defined: below target (termination risk), on target, and above target (upside).

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the primary formula for building great software is to identify the outcome users want and then remove all friction between where they currently are and achieving that outcome. He uses the iPhone as the canonical example: Steve Jobs identified what people wanted from a phone and systematically removed friction until a baby could operate it.
pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi describes a magical product as one where complexity happens invisibly in the background, and the member experiences only the frictionless output. He cites a $250M product executive's framework: the product must feel magical, meaning all hard work is invisible to the end user.

pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi claims that the specificity of instruction required is inversely correlated with the skill level of the recipient: a low-skill person needs every sub-step explained (e.g., what a browser is, how to type), while a high-skill person can execute from a single high-level directive. Entrepreneurs misidentify this as a motivation problem when it is actually a skill-gap problem.
